behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-signature-verifier plus handler-to-queue handoff (`401` and `not forwarded`). Unit tests of only the HMAC helper miss wiring bugs (e.g., wrong raw-body bytes passed). Rejected higher levels: behavior text only requires signature check and forwarding suppression, not real OS/network/subprocess or AI output.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook handler through the real HTTP routing/middleware stack in-process with a real raw body payload and bad `X-Signature-256`; assert `401` and assert queue write method is never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is explicit cross-module ordering (`written to the persistent queue before ... 200`). Needs real handler+queue module wiring and transactional ordering, not just function logic. Rejected System: behavior does not require real external network/subprocess; local persistent backend suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process server + real queue adapter (backed by local test DB/filesystem). Instrument order (or transaction boundaries) to prove durable enqueue completes before response flush; fail if response is sent first.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is orchestration between request handler, queueing, and async downstream worker. Unit-only tests can miss await/Promise wiring mistakes across modules. Rejected System: no requirement for real OS/subprocess/network timing beyond in-process async control.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream consumer in-process; make consumer block (e.g., unresolved promise for several seconds). Assert HTTP response is returned quickly while consumer still running.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Failure depends on real wall-clock latency under concurrency and event-loop/resource contention. In-process fakes or mocked timers can hide real scheduling/backpressure failures. Rejected lower levels: behavior text explicitly requires `within 3 seconds wall-clock` under `slow and concurrent` load.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process, drive concurrent HTTP webhook requests over loopback while earlier events are intentionally slow in downstream processing, and measure end-to-end response latency; assert P95/P100 (per spec) stays under 3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup is a state-handoff/persistence boundary problem (handler + idempotency store/queue), not single-function logic. Unit tests can miss uniqueness checks wired incorrectly. Rejected System: behavior does not require real external infra beyond local persistence semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Use real handler + real dedup/persistent queue modules with local DB/filesystem. Send same event ID twice; assert first enqueue succeeds and second does not create an additional queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is module interaction: queue capacity error propagation to HTTP mapping (`queue reports ... full` -> `503` + header). Unit-only mapping test can miss real error-type propagation across modules. Rejected System: no real OS/network/subprocess dependency required.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with real queue module configured to small capacity, fill it, then send another request and assert HTTP `503` plus valid `Retry-After` header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets queue component behavior with real persistence backend (local DB/filesystem), beyond pure function logic. Mocked queue would trivially pass and hide backend constraint bugs. Rejected System: behavior text focuses on queue enforcement semantics, not external network/subprocess/OS-only effects.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue implementation with a concrete capacity (e.g., 10), enqueue to limit, then enqueue one more and assert a deterministic capacity-exceeded rejection and unchanged persisted depth.
